title: Dungeons & Dragons: Dragon Quest - RetroLook
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zDtacZ_qT0Y

Daniel (@dungeondive) examines Dragon Quest, TSR's other early HeroQuest answer — but where Dragon Strike leans board game, this leans hard toward RPG, a genuine on-ramp into full D&D that even teaches the THAC0 system (better, he says, than the actual manuals did). Its greatest asset is the art: drawn from TSR's vast, gorgeous fantasy library, it stands in stark contrast to the artless modern WotC adventure games, and would have set a young player's imagination alight.

He tours the box: a HeroQuest-style featureless board the adventure dictates layouts onto, hero cards, an enormous variety of monster standees (more enemy variety than any modern crawl in one box), cleric/wizard spell decks, item and treasure cards each with helpful art, and high-quality dice. Combat is THAC0 reworded more clearly. His criticisms are pointed: only three adventures (it needed at least six), and no character-creation or adventure-building rules — a third book would have made it a complete intro to roleplaying. A strong, art-rich gateway game hobbled by a thin adventure book.

----

Dragon Quest poured TSR's stunning fantasy art across every card, while many modern adventure games ship near-artless. How much does evocative art shape whether a game pulls you into its world?
